planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions 2–3 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic soil in partial to deep shade; myoga is one of the few edible gingers that actively prefers shade and performs poorly in full sun in hot climates.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to deep shade; myoga is the only ginger species that genuinely prefers shade, making it one of the most valuable edible plants for the shaded food garden. The edible parts are the flower buds and young shoots that emerge directly from the ground in late summer and fall — they are sliced and used as a fresh garnish in Japanese cuisine with a mild ginger-citrus flavor. The tall, arching reed-like stems make attractive foliage throughout summer. In zone 7–11 the roots are winter-hardy and re-emerge each spring, slowly spreading to form a clump; divide every 3–4 years to maintain vigor. An outstanding shade-garden edible for Gulf Coast gardens where deep shade under trees is otherwise difficult to plant productively.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in partial to full shade; cardamom requires consistently moist soil and high humidity — it is not suited to dry climates or exposed windy sites. In zone 10–11 only.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, acidic, well-drained soil in partial to deep shade — cardamom naturally grows as an understory plant in the humid rainforests of southern India and is one of the world's most valuable spices after vanilla and saffron. The large, aromatic leaves are beautiful throughout the growing season; fragrant flowers are produced on separate ground-level stems and develop into the prized seed pods. Cardamom is only reliably hardy in zone 10–12 but makes an outstanding container plant in zone 8–9 that overwinters indoors. It requires high humidity, consistent moisture, and shade from afternoon sun — an excellent fit for the shaded, humid conditions of South Florida and the Gulf Coast. Harvest pods when they are pale green before they open.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions 2–4 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; butterfly ginger is invasive in Florida, Hawaii, and parts of the Gulf Coast — consider this before planting and do not plant near natural areas. Container planting prevents spread.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich soil in partial shade; butterfly ginger is among the most intensely fragrant of all flowering plants — the pure white, butterfly-shaped flowers in summer and fall fill the garden with a heady sweet fragrance that carries 20–30 feet in still evening air. It is the national flower of Cuba and is widely grown throughout the tropics for its extraordinary perfume, used to scent garlands and leis. The tall, handsome reed-like stems 4–6 feet tall with broad, lance-shaped leaves make it an imposing accent or screening plant in shaded areas. In zone 8–9 the foliage dies back in winter but the roots resprout reliably from established rhizomes. Note that this species is listed as invasive in Florida, Hawaii, and the Pacific islands — plant responsibly, do not plant near waterways or natural areas, and contain the rhizomes if needed.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade; in zone 8 the stems die back in hard freezes but roots resprout from established clumps. The variegated cultivar 'Variegata' offers striking yellow-striped foliage year-round.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade — shell ginger is one of the most ornamental foliage plants for the shaded Gulf Coast garden, producing broad, lush, glossy leaves 18–24 inches long in dense clumps of upright 5–8 foot stems. Established clumps produce pendulous clusters of waxy, shell-like white flowers tipped with yellow and rose in spring, highly aromatic and used in tropical garlands. The foliage is also strongly aromatic when crushed. The cultivar 'Variegata' has striking yellow-green striped leaves even more ornamental than the species and is widely grown in zone 8–11 as a bold accent or container plant. In zone 8, top growth is damaged by hard freezes but roots resprout from established clumps. The leaves are used in Southeast Asian cooking to wrap and flavor rice and fish.

planting.note: Direct sow seed on the soil surface (seeds need light to germinate) in moist, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade just before last frost; jewelweed self-sows prolifically and typically establishes as a self-sustaining colony once introduced — no further planting is needed after the first year.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface of moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade in early spring; jewelweed is a tall native annual of moist woodlands, stream banks, and swampy areas throughout most of North America — one of the few native annuals that thrives in deep shade and moist conditions. The orange, trumpet-shaped flowers are uniquely constructed with a curved spur and are the primary nectar source for ruby-throated hummingbirds in eastern North America during their southward fall migration. The ripe seed pods burst explosively when touched (hence 'touch-me-not'), flinging seeds up to 3–4 feet. Fresh crushed stems and leaves are widely used as a folk remedy to relieve the itch of poison ivy contact, with some evidence supporting anti-inflammatory compounds in the juice. Jewelweed is a powerful self-sower that naturalizes in moist, shaded areas and requires no care once established.
